beats.text: Earlier chapters taught techniques. This page reorganizes the same material by the work you actually start with.
Nobody opens a model thinking about self-consistency. You open it holding a contract, two spreadsheets, or a draft due Thursday.
The working claim is that most knowledge work reaching a model fits one of six task shapes.
Each shape is hardened against a different failure: omission, fabricated symmetry, motivated chains, fake arithmetic, self-applause, lossy compression.
Every skeleton builds on the five-anchor scaffold: role, context, task, format, constraints.
Read each entry as when, skeleton, filled, hardening. Loosen these for low-stakes work, never for documents others rely on.
Pattern P1 turns one long document into rows: every obligation, every control, every deadline.
The tell is the word every. Completeness is the success criterion, and a summary is licensed to drop things.
Every row carries a source reference and a verbatim quote. A row you cannot cite is a row you must not write.
Extraction fails invisibly. Twenty-three confident rows tell you nothing about the four that were silently dropped.
Under context pressure, models lose the middle of long documents first, so a zero-yield section list makes silence auditable.
Past roughly thirty pages, run the skeleton once per section and concatenate, since completeness per chunk is cheap.

concepts.title: Compare & Contrast — N documents → discrepancy log

beats.text: Pattern P2 handles artifacts that are supposed to agree: a policy and its procedure, a contract and its renewal.
The deliverable is a keyed, cited discrepancy log, not an essay about themes. The first question is always where exactly.
The failure is fabricated symmetry: handed a comparison table, models fill it even when the documents mostly agree.

visual.sub: Filling a table that should be empty

beats.text: Position bias also creeps in: the document pasted last sits closer to the instruction and quietly wins ties.
An item present in one document and absent from the other is a discrepancy, logged as missing, not buried in a footnote.

visual.sub: Missing in A / Missing in B

beats.text: The do-not-manufacture clause legalizes the empty table, so agreement can be reported in a single honest line.
Pattern P3 produces a chain of judgments a reviewer can audit step by step: risk assessments, control walkthroughs.
The conclusion matters less than the visible path to it. An approver will read the middle, not just the end.
Each step states the criterion, the evidence, the judgment, and the confidence, in that fixed order.
The filled example walks a payment workflow through five controls, including a fifty-thousand-euro second-approver limit.
The failure is the motivated chain: the model settles on a verdict early and back-fills steps that point to it.
Evidence before judgment exploits field order as computation order, and no-smoothing forces a failed step into sentence one.

concepts.title: Find & Quantify — data → exceptions, counts, aggregates

beats.text: Pattern P4 takes data in and returns answers with units: how many, which ones, how far over.
Two careful humans on the same rules would produce identical output. There is no judgment in the answer, only in the rules.
The filled scan screens two hundred fourteen card lines against three rules, including a split-transaction check.
Boundaries are decided in advance: exactly five hundred euros passes, while five hundred point zero one breaches.
The failure is plausible arithmetic, because models see tokens, not numbers, so counts can be confidently wrong.
Reconciliation makes errors detectable, but beyond a few dozen rows, treat the prompt as a spec and run the query.
Pattern P5 is for high-stakes outbound writing where the second pass is the point: board papers, regulator letters.
One prompt produces three passes: a draft, a rubric-scored critique, and a revision that fixes only what was flagged.
The filled example answers an audit finding where access reviews ran forty-seven days late in two consecutive quarters.
The critique must find at least three concrete weaknesses with quoted passages. Reads well overall is a failed critique.
The failure is the rubber-stamp critique: a model grading its own work drifts toward applause, the self-preference bias.
Self-critique improves structure and tone reliably, less so facts. When facts are load-bearing, split the passes.

concepts.title: Translate & Reframe — same truth, new container

beats.text: Pattern P6 changes the container while the content survives: sixty pages to one, engineering register to board register.
The deliverable is defined by its invariants: the numbers, caveats, and locked terms that must come through intact.
The filled example compresses a sixty-page policy into a one-page brief with three headed sections of four sentences each.
Locked terminology renders exactly: impact tolerance, not risk appetite; important business service, not critical process.

visual.sub: A synonym is a new term

beats.text: Compression drops caveats first, since they are statistically peripheral and legally central, so an omitted ledger logs each loss.
Long translations drift, so spot-check the last page, not the first, because drift accumulates by the ninth occurrence.
The Quick Library lists twelve recurring jobs, each condensed to a single instruction you can paste and extend.
Examples include a runbook from a transcript, monthly commentary from CSVs, and a meeting-to-decisions log.
Each entry carries the failure its full-length version would have hardened against, in a third column.
The anomaly-scan one-liner warns that above about fifty rows you should have the model write the query instead.
When a one-liner starts feeding a decision, promote it to the full six-pattern treatment with proper hardening.
Every watch-out is one of the six failure modes in different clothes. The library is finite because the failures are.
The Pattern Picker maps ten everyday requests to their shape, and just as important, names the tempting wrong one.
The most common error is not a bad prompt, it is the wrong shape: reasoning where you needed a diff.
Reconciling two spreadsheets is P2, not P3: you want a line-by-line diff, not confident prose about why they differ.
Summarizing a sixty-page policy for execs is P6, not P1: a forty-row clause table is faithful and unreadable.
The mapping is hand-written and opinionated. Disagree with it after you have been burned, not before.
Every pattern here is one prompt, one pass, one output you inspect. Volume IV is when the model runs the loop itself.
